Analysis of  the GW170817 kilonova has relied pri-
marily on radiative transfer modeling (e.g., Kasen et
al. 2017; Chornock et al. 2017; Watson et al. 2019;
Gillanders et al. 2022, 2024; Sneppen et al. 2024) and
analytic/semi-analytic models (e.g., Cowperthwaite et
al. 2017; Drout et al. 2017; Kasliwal et al. 2017; Villar
et al. 2017; Rosswog et al. 2018; Waxman et al. 2018;
Hotokezaka & Nakar 2020). Analytic treatments have
been especially common in studying the multi-band light
curves. An important ingredient in light curve models
is the radioactive heating rate, q˙(t). This q˙ (energy per
unit time per unit mass) describes how much energy is

of line transitions, especially in the UV and optical. For

However, in reality, the opacity may be strongly time-

al. 2013; Tanaka et al. 2020; Fontes et al. 2020; Kato
et al. 2024), as evidenced by the color evolution in the
GW170817 kilonova.
In Vieira et al.  (2023) and Vieira et al.  (2024),
we introduce the Spectroscopic r-Process Abundance
Retrieval  for Kilonovae (SPARK) software, which can
be used to directly infer key kilonova ejecta proper-
ties from spectra,  including r-process abundance pat-
terns.  SPARK fits kilonova spectra using the Monte
Carlo radiative transfer code TARDIS (Kerzendorf &
Sim 2014)  through a Bayesian approach,  acceler-
ated by the Bayesian approximate posterior estima-
tion scheme  (Kandasamy et  al.  2017)  implemented
in approxposterior (Fleming & VanderPlas 2018).
TARDIS takes grids of  elemental  abundance patterns
from nuclear reaction network calculations as an input,
enabling us to retrieve the element-by-element abun-.

2.1.  Spectral Inference with SPARK
In Vieira et al. (2023) (hereafter V23) and Vieira et
al. (2024) (hereafter V24), we introduce SPARK, our tool
for fitting the spectra of kilonovae to infer the element-
by-element abundances and other key properties of the
kilonova ejecta. In those works, we fit the observed spec-
tra of the GW170817 kilonova (Pian et al. 2017; Smartt
et al. 2017) at 1.4, 2.4, and 3.4 days post-merger; here
we also include a new fit to the spectrum at 4.4 days.
Figure 2 shows our best-fit spectra at each of  these
epochs. Tables 1 and 2 present our best-fit parameters
for the inference at each epoch, including the luminos-

We use the nuclear reaction network calculations of
Wanajo (2018) as our source of abundance patterns,
which parametrize the abundances as a function of elec-
tron fraction Ye, expansion velocity vexp, and specific
entropy s. Spectral modeling with TARDIS (Kerzendorf
& Sim 2014) takes in these abundance patterns as an
input, enabling us to directly infer the abundances as a
function of the Ye, vexp, and s parameters. Our best-
fit abundance patterns at the four spectral epochs are
shown in Figure 3.
The spectra at 1.4 and 2.4 days poster-merger are
best described by a single-component, bluer (higher-Ye)
ejecta that contains a higher abundance of  lighter r-
process elements.  At 3.4 days, and persisting at 4.4
days, an additional redder,  lower-Ye ejecta component
with an high abundance of heavier elements emerges.
This redder component has a substantial abundance of
lanthanides (atomic number Z  [57, 71]), characterized
∈
by high opacities in the ultraviolet and optical (Tanaka
et al. 2020; Kato et al. 2024), leading to the redder color
observed in the spectra.  Notably, our inference does
not suggest a strong radial gradient in the composition:
the blue and red components nearly completely overlap
radially at 3.4 and 4.4 days, although TARDIS is only
one-dimensional and thus we cannot comment on the
angular distribution.  This near-complete overlap also
means that the mass is roughly equiparitioned between
the two components. We refer the reader to V24 and
other relevant works (Watson et al. 2019; Gillanders et
al. 2022; Sneppen et al. 2024) for in-depth discussions
on the evolution of these parameters and their imprint
on the emergent spectra. We now turn our attention to
the quantities of greatest interest for the light curves:
the radioactive heating rates and opacities.
2.2.  Inferring Radioactive Heating Rates
We use the set of parametric trajectories from the
nuclear network calculations of Wanajo (2018) to ob-
tain the composition and radioactive heating rates at
given our inferred Ye,  vexp,  and s.

Given a nuclear
network calculation, tracer particles propagate through
a plasma to trace the composition of the ejecta as a
function of three parameters:  Ye, vexp, and s in the
plasma.  Crucially, Wanajo (2018) compute the com-
position and heating rates as a function of time, from

2.3.  Inferring Time and Wavelength-Dependent
Opacities
2.3.1. Expansion Opacities
Given  a  sample  from our  SPARK  posteriors  for
Ye, vexp and s, we also have all the ingredients to ex-
tract an opacity. TARDIS uses the wavelength-dependent
expansion opacity formalism to describe bound-bound
opacities in a homologously-expanding ejecta. For each
sample in our posterior, we instantiate a plasma and per-
form radiative transfer under the assumptions of local
thermodynamic equilibrium (LTE). Level populations
and ionization are computed using the Saha-Boltzmann
equations. This computation yields the Sobolev optical
depth τSob for all relevant atomic transitions. We then
compute the wavelength-dependent expansion opacity
κexp(λ), which sums transitions in successive wavelength

Figure 6 shows the expansion opacities inferred at 1.4,
2.4, 3.4, and 4.4 days, along with uncertainties. To ob-
tain a reasonable opacity, we must use some total ejecta
mass beyond what is contained in the line-forming re-
gion. We thus compute opacities for 0.01 M⊙ of ejecta
between 0.1c and 0.4c at 1.5 days post-merger,  in line
with other opacity calculations (e.g., Tanaka et al. 2020).
To set the temperatures for the opacity calculations, we
first perform a blackbody fit to the entire spectral range
at each epoch and find 4800, 3400, 2900, and 2700 K
at 1.4,  2.4,  3.4,  and 4.4 days,  respectively.  How-
ever,  Sneppen et al.  (2024) point out  that  such a
simple fit  to the entire spectral  range does not ac-
count  for deviations  from a blackbody due to emis-
sion/absorption, which can bias this measurement to
colder temperatures at early times. Fitting more care-
fully with additional parameters to capture the absorp-
tion/emission, Sneppen et al. (2024) find temperatures
of 5440, 3940, 3420, and 3330 K at 1.4, 2.4, 3.4, and
4.4 days, respectively. We thus use these more carefully-
measured temperatures in our calculations and through-
out all of our analyses.

We compute the expansion opacities in a single shell
of material spanning the inferred vinner and vouter, i.e.,
at a single density and temperature. In all calculations,
we compute the opacities for a single composition.  In
the case of the two-component ejecta, we compute the
opacity for the mass-weighted and summed mixture of
the red and blue components.

This approach is distinct
from the more common practice of computing the opac-
ities for different components separately and/or com-
puting emission from distinct components and summing
those emissions. Our approach here thus captures the
important physics of reprocessing, whereby one compo-
nent can absorb the emission of the other and reemit
it,  leading to a diversity of kilonovae (e.g., Kawaguchi
et al. 2020). This approach yields a single wavelength-
dependent opacity at each epoch.
The expansion opacities in Figure 6 display the strong
wavelength dependence that is expected.  At 3.4 and
4.4 days,  the opacities are overall higher due to the
larger abundance of open d-shell  (Z  [40,  58] and
∈
[72,  80])  and f-shell  elements,  especially  the  lan-
thanides. The opacities are highest at the shortest wave-
lengths ≲10, 000 Å, due to the presence of these lan-
thanides with high opacities at these wavelengths. There
is an additional peak in opacity at  15, 000 Å, which we
∼
attribute to cerium (58Ce, specifically Ce II) in V24.
2.3.2. Time-dependent Gray Opacities
With these expansion opacities in hand, to obtain a
wavelength-independent (‘gray’) opacity that can be in-
gested by our light curve model, we compute the Planck
mean opacity. This Planck mean opacity is the mean
of the expansion opacity over wavelength, weighted by
the Planck function at some temperature T .  Impor-
tantly,  in V23 and V24, we find that our spectral fits

2.4. Light Curve Model and Inputs
We use the light curve model of Hotokezaka & Nakar
7
(2020).  This 1-dimensional model computes the lumi-
nosity output by homologously-expanding spherically-
symmetric ejecta that is heated by radioactive decays,
producing thermal emission.  The ejecta is character-
ized by some number of concentric radial shells, each
at its own density ρ, velocity v, and opacity κ.  Some
total mass Mej  is distributed according to a time- and
velocity-dependent power-law density profile, ρ(t, v) =
−3  β

the shells according to the radioactive heating rate q˙(t),
multiplied by a thermalization efficiency ϵth(t) which de-
scribes how efficiently the energy contained in radioac-
tive decay products is deposited into the ejecta.  The
luminosity of all shells is computed and summed to pro-
duce the output light curve. Below, we describe which of
the model parameters are set according to our inference
on the spectra, which parameters are fixed, and which
parameters are fit.

malization of the products of alpha decays, beta decays,

(2018), we partition the energy carried by the products
of different decays as:  α particles at 5%, β particles
(electrons/positrons) at 20%, gamma-rays at 40%, and

of the thermalization efficiency come from the depen-
dence of ϵth on ρ(t, v), and so each shell in the velocity
array is then described by its own unique thermalization
efficiency.

Given the wide range in masses we explore in
this work and our array of shells at different velocities,
this general approach is more appropriate than the oft-
used Barnes et al. (2016) thermalization efficiency, which
is provided in analytic form for only masses in the range
0.001 to 0.05 M⊙ and is only dependent on time.
We also provide the gray opacity in each shell, as
required by the light curve model.  In practice, to fit
the suite of light curve data from the kilonova, we re-
quire a light curve model that is smooth in time and
can be evaluated outside of the discrete times at which
we perform spectral inference. We therefore also require
a smooth opacity κ(t, v). Because spectral modeling is
sensitive only to the material above the photosphere in
the range (vinner, vouter), we only infer the opacities in
these velocity ranges at each epoch (2.3, Figure 8). As
the ejecta expands,  the photosphere recedes, decreas-
ing the inferred vinner and enabling measurement of the
opacity deeper into the ejecta, but there remains some
fraction of the ejecta which is unseen. Fitting in velocity
is complicated by our not knowing these opacities be-
low the photosphere, where most of the ejecta mass lies,
especially at 1.4 and 2.4 days. Furthermore, although
we infer multiple components in the ejecta, our spectral
fits show no evidence for strong radial dependence in
composition from 0.17c to 0.35c for these components.
Finally, we only have two data points for the opacity of
a red component.

With our tool SPARK, we fit the optically-thick, early-
time, optical/infrared spectra of the GW170817 kilonova

a et al.
at 1.4, 2.4, 3.4, and 4.4 days post-merger. We directly
measure the element-by-element abundance pattern of
the kilonova ejecta, with uncertainties. Leveraging the
results of parametric nuclear reaction network calcula-
tions, we turn our measurement of the kilonova ejecta’s
composition into a measurement of the time-dependent
radioactive heating rate q˙(t) and the wavelength-, time-
, and velocity-dependent opacity κ(λ, t, v) of the ejecta.
We report in detail these heating rates in Tables 1 and 2,
and the opacities in Tables 3 and 4 (Appendix B).
We input these opacities and heating rates into a sim-
ple kilonova light curve model, and fit the multi-band
light curves of the GW170817 kilonova to infer the to-
tal ejecta mass—a quantity which cannot be obtained
from spectral modeling—while accurately reproducing
the observed light curves. We find a total ejecta mass of
+0.04
0.11−0.02 M⊙ between 0.1c and 0.4c in our light curve
fit. This large ejecta mass can be explained by invok-
ing a hypermassive magnetar remnant which survives
for  0.01  0.5 s post-merger,  launching as much as
∼  −
0.05 M⊙ of blue ejecta material from its surface for
∼
longer lifetimes. This component coexists with a red ac-
cretion disk wind, which may be accelerated to higher
velocities by a large-scale poloidal magnetic field in the
disk, associated with the magnetar. Tidal ejecta could
also contribute to this red component.
With this bridge between the spectral and time do-
mains, we put forward a self-consistent and coherent
model for the kilonova. Our spectral inference provides
the detailed composition and physical conditions of the
ejecta, while our light curve modeling is required to mea-
sure the total ejecta mass. This combination thus en-
ables us to more robustly determine the physical origins
of the ejecta. Contemporaneous spectroscopic and pho-
tometric observations will be critical to fully illuminat-
ing and understanding the diverse properties of a future
sample of kilonovae.
